news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking advances in quantum physics and materials science, including experimental verification of quantum simulations, novel discoveries about liquid behavior, and innovative developments in autonomous laboratory research.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires en physique quantique et en science des matériaux, notamment la vérification expérimentale de simulations quantiques, de nouvelles découvertes sur le comportement des liquides et des développements innovants dans le domaine de la recherche en laboratoire autonome.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en física cuántica y ciencia de los materiales, entre los que se incluyen la verificación experimental de simulaciones cuánticas, nuevos descubrimientos sobre el comportamiento de los líquidos y avances innovadores en la investigación de laboratorio autónoma.

articles.title: Inside the ‘self-driving’ lab revolution
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00974-2AI-powered robotic tools are muscling in on tasks typically done by humans. What does the future hold?
articles.content:
The robot scientist Eve was built at the University of Manchester, UK, before being moved to Sweden.Credit: Courtesy of Ross D. King

Measuring 5 metres square by 3 metres high, Eve takes up at least half of the floor space in the laboratory it now calls home.

The robotic platform at the Chalmers University of Technology in Gothenburg, Sweden, is the brainchild of autonomous-lab pioneer Ross King. It is powered by artificial intelligence, self-driving and “fairly quiet”, King says. But it’s also fast. Working at full speed, Eve’s robotic arm can move a few metres per second, with a positional accuracy of a fraction of a millimetre. The team usually runs Eve slower than that — otherwise, King says, “it’s too scary”.

Eve automates the process of early-stage drug design. One of Eve’s early achievements came in 2018, around three years after it was created, when it identified that the common antimicrobial compound triclosan can target an enzyme that is crucial to the survival of Plasmodium malaria parasites during their dormant phase in the liver1. To do this, Eve independently screened some 1,600 chemicals and modelled how their structure related to their activity to predict which ones were worth testing. King and his group armed the robot with background knowledge and a machine-learning framework for developing hypotheses. Eve then used those elements to design experiments to test these hypotheses and, crucially, performed them itself. The finding gave researchers a potential route to fighting treatment-resistant malaria. “It’s trying to make the scientific method in a machine,” says King.

Will self-driving 'robot labs' replace biologists? Paper sparks debate

In 2009, King used Eve’s predecessor to probe some of the 10–15% of yeast genes with unknown functions2. He named the system Adam — a reference to both the biblical character and the eighteenth-century economist Adam Smith, who was a strong proponent of industrial mechanization. King sees parallels in the future of science.

“A lot of biology is done like craft work,” King says: a lab with a principal investigator, postdocs and students operates much like an artisan works with their apprentices. Self-driving labs, by contrast, are more similar to a production line. As a result, “science will be done differently, like in a factory”, he adds.

The technology is still in its infancy, and most of the advances so far have been incremental. But as the field encroaches on parts of the scientific process that are typically done by people — absorbing the literature, planning experiments, analysing data and deciding what hypothesis to test next — researchers will have to grapple with what the developments mean for the future of the lab.

The anatomy of a self-driving lab

Many sectors, from agriculture to surgery, are starting to embrace the promise of AI-powered robotics. Korean car manufacturer Hyundai, for example, announced in January that it will deploy tens of thousands of autonomous humanoid robots in its manufacturing plants, and that they will be completing complex car-assembly work by 2030.

Industrial labs and centralized lab facilities have been using robots to speed up liquid handling and sample analysis since the mid-1980s. But self-driving labs can go much further. Blending AI, robotics and automated instrumentation, these platforms can design and perform experiments with minimal human input.

Self-driving laboratories, advanced immunotherapies and five more technologies to watch in 2025

Adam is equipped with a freezer full of mutant yeast strains and the chemicals needed to measure cellular growth under various conditions. It also hosts three incubators, a centrifuge, two barcode readers, seven cameras and 20 environmental sensors. After being given an overarching goal by its human handlers, it independently develops hypotheses and then tests them, performing experiments much faster than a human could.

Hiring a student for the job would probably have been cheaper, King admits. But his newest robot, Genesis, will be able to do enough experiments to make the process economically feasible3. King estimates that Genesis will cost £1 million (US$1.3 million) to build — the same price as Adam or Eve individually — but he estimates that it will eventually be at least an order of magnitude cheaper than human labour. King plans to use the system — which occupies one-fifth of floor space than Eve does — to model how genes, proteins and small molecules interact in cells. Part of that will involve taking around 10,000 mass-spectrometry measurements each day.

Chemist and computer scientist Alán Aspuru-Guzik at the University of Toronto in Canada supervises a fleet of 50 self-driving autonomous robots across several labs and universities. Known as the Acceleration Consortium, it is funded by a grant worth Can$200 million (US$146 million).

Can robotic lab assistants speed up your work?

One of his former postdocs, chemist Gabe Gomes, went on to set up his own autonomous lab at Carnegie Mellon University (CMU) in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, which he calls Coscientist4. It is part of a new generation of systems that “allows users to give instructions or [make] requests in plain English”, says Gomes. Coscientist is driven by the large language model (LLM) GPT-4 and can interpret scientific problems, collect relevant information from web and document searches, plan experiments and interface with robotic lab hardware to perform them. This is done either on external automation platforms or by using the CMU Cloud Lab — a remote-controlled, fully automated research facility built by CMU and Emerald Cloud Lab, a biotechnology company in Austin, Texas.

Coscientist has designed and run palladium-catalysed organic reactions to find the best reagents and conditions4. But it has applications across a wide range of fields, Gomes says. “It’s really field-agnostic. And as [AI] models get better, the problems that we can tackle are much greater.”

Global and cost-effective

One researcher hoping to leverage this technology is John Gregoire, chief autonomous-science officer at Lila Sciences, a start-up firm in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

With around 22,000 square metres of automated lab space at its AI Science Factory (AISF), the company plans to provide research and development services to pharmaceutical companies, materials-science firms and other research-intensive organizations. This year, it received about £500,000 from the UK government’s Advanced Research and Innovation Agency to test whether its self-driving robot — AI NanoScientist — can synthesize and improve the stability of colloidal nanoparticles, tiny particles suspended in a liquid medium.

Researchers at Lila Sciences hope to provide services to research-intensive organizations.Credit: Cody O’Loughlin/New York Times/Redux/eyevine

A similar venture, Periodic Labs, was launched in 2025 in San Francisco, California. It was co-founded by San Francisco-based Liam Fedus, a creator of ChatGPT at US tech firm OpenAI, and Ekin Dogus Cubuk, who previously led materials and chemistry research at Google DeepMind. Periodic Labs has developed an automated materials-synthesis lab that can mix powders, heat them in a furnace and characterize the products. The company aims to perform 1,000 experiments each day, but Cubuk says that success will depend not on throughput, but on how well the LLM can analyse results to progress to further experiments. Similar ventures are popping up around the world, including LabGenius in London. Its discovery platform, called EVA, combines AI and robotic automation to develop complex therapeutic antibodies. Novartis, a pharmaceutical company in Basel, Switzerland, has developed a platform called MicroCycle, which can autonomously synthesize, purify and test compounds, analyse the data and choose new compounds to synthesize5. And an AI-powered robotic chemist called ChemAgents, developed by researchers at the University of Science and Technology of China in Hefei, helped its creators to discover functional materials and optimize light-activated organic reactions6.

Evidence is mounting that autonomous labs can be more cost-effective than conventional approaches are. For example, scientists at OpenAI and Ginkgo Bioworks, a biotech company in Cambridge, Massachusetts, tested more than 30,000 experimental conditions over six months. They demonstrated that blending Gingko’s Reconfigurable Automation Cart cloud lab with the GPT-5 LLM reduces the cost-per-gram of protein production in a test tube by 40% relative to state-of-the-art methods7. The experiment improved protein yield by 27%.

Humans welcome

That’s not to say robots can do everything humans can. “You can’t put a robot arm into a cage and catch a mouse in a corner, for instance. Human dexterity is amazing compared to current robots,” says King. Gregoire echoes the point, noting that some processes are simply too expensive to automate for now.

‘Set it and forget it’: automated lab uses AI and robotics to improve proteins

But AI-powered robots are starting to perform more-complex experiments than standard automated systems can handle, which generally involve single-stage syntheses. One robot in Aspuru-Guzik’s Acceleration Consortium, for instance, is working on multistep methods that would otherwise be difficult to automate because the desired compounds must be purified at each step — a complex, delicate task that requires nuanced judgement. Solve that problem and “the world is yours”, Aspuru-Guzik says, “because then you can, in principle, automate any chemical reaction”. Using the consortium’s SDL7 ‘scale-up’ automated lab at the University of British Columbia in Vancouver, Canada, his team is working with the pharmaceutical giant Bristol Myers Squibb in Princeton, New Jersey, on a platform that can separate mixtures of liquids. It can operate autonomously to prepare samples, measure pH and analyse the different liquid layers.

Some of these systems even have ‘eyes’. In December 2025, Aspuru-Guzik’s group published a guide on how to use a simple webcam to empower self-driving labs to watch as experiments progress and respond to what happens — in this case, the high-throughput synthesis of highly tunable, porous lattice structures known as metal–organic frameworks8. “With the computer eyes, [the robot] was able to actually see what happens in the reaction, and then actually act upon it,” he explains. It could identify plates on which products had crystallized and select only those for characterization, increasing efficiency.

Incremental improvements

For now, researchers at Lila Sciences are moving towards fully autonomous operation of the AISF for developing messenger RNA therapeutics and catalysts, but the system still relies on human input to validate AI predictions. Fedus and Cubuk say that Periodic Labs is likewise easing into the process by automating pieces of it to ensure that the AI’s proposed syntheses make sense. “It’s a very iterative process,” says Fedus.

Indeed, the types of hypothesis that self-driving labs can test for now are “relatively constrained”, says King, and focus mainly on incremental improvements. “They optimize compounds in a drug assay, or they optimize some material for a battery or solar panel.” Typically, this is accomplished using Bayesian optimization, a method that uses probabilistic modelling to select the experiments and conditions most likely to improve current results. “Bayesian optimization is awesome and powerful,” says Gomes. But Coscientist’s LLM-guided optimization technique could make things even better. The system is pretrained with chemical knowledge, giving it an edge over the conventional Bayesian approach, Gomes says.
articles.reasoning: The article describes significant advances in AI-powered self-driving laboratories that automate and accelerate scientific research, particularly in drug discovery and materials science. These autonomous labs have broad implications for improving research efficiency, reducing costs, and potentially accelerating breakthroughs that benefit society at large. The story is focused on a clear technological innovation with detailed examples and its potential future impact, meeting criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Robots are running labs to speed up science and drug discovery
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have started using robots powered by artificial intelligence (AI) to run laboratories. These robots can design and carry out experiments on their own, which could change how research is done.

**What happened** – A robot scientist named Eve, built in the UK and now working in Sweden, can quickly test many chemicals to find new medicines. Eve once discovered a way to fight malaria by testing 1,600 chemicals without human help. Other robots like Adam and Genesis are also being developed to study genes and molecules. Some labs now use AI robots that understand instructions in plain English and can plan experiments themselves.

**Impact** – These self-driving labs can do experiments faster and cheaper than humans. For example, one robot tested over 30,000 conditions and helped reduce costs by 40% while improving protein production. Robots can perform complex tasks like watching experiments with cameras and adjusting steps, which helps make research more efficient and accurate.

**What's next step** – Researchers are working to make these labs fully autonomous, meaning robots will handle almost all parts of experiments. They are improving robots’ skills to do more complex tasks and hope to use them for developing new medicines and materials faster. Some companies are already setting up large robot labs to offer services to other researchers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI-powered robots running self-driving labs are transforming scientific research by making experiments faster, cheaper, and more precise.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des robots dirigent des laboratoires pour accélérer la science et la découverte de médicaments
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques utilisent des robots dotés d’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour gérer des laboratoires. Ces robots peuvent concevoir et réaliser des expériences de façon autonome, ce qui pourrait changer la manière de faire la recherche.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un robot scientifique nommé Eve, construit au Royaume-Uni et maintenant en Suède, peut tester rapidement de nombreux produits chimiques pour trouver de nouveaux médicaments. Eve a par exemple découvert une méthode pour lutter contre le paludisme en testant 1 600 produits chimiques sans aide humaine. D’autres robots comme Adam et Genesis sont aussi développés pour étudier les gènes et les molécules. Certains laboratoires utilisent maintenant des robots IA qui comprennent des instructions en langage simple et peuvent planifier eux-mêmes les expériences.

**Impact** – Ces laboratoires autonomes peuvent réaliser des expériences plus rapidement et à moindre coût que les humains. Par exemple, un robot a testé plus de 30 000 conditions et a aidé à réduire les coûts de 40 % tout en améliorant la production de protéines. Les robots peuvent aussi accomplir des tâches complexes comme observer les expériences avec des caméras et ajuster les étapes, ce qui rend la recherche plus efficace et précise.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs travaillent à rendre ces laboratoires entièrement autonomes, où les robots gèrent presque toutes les parties des expériences. Ils améliorent les capacités des robots pour réaliser des tâches plus complexes et espèrent les utiliser pour développer plus rapidement de nouveaux médicaments et matériaux. Certaines entreprises mettent déjà en place de grands laboratoires robotisés pour offrir leurs services à d’autres chercheurs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les robots pilotés par IA qui dirigent des laboratoires autonomes transforment la recherche scientifique en rendant les expériences plus rapides, moins coûteuses et plus précises.
articles.personality_title_es: Robots dirigen laboratorios para acelerar la ciencia y el descubrimiento de medicamentos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos están usando robots con inteligencia artificial (IA) para manejar laboratorios. Estos robots pueden diseñar y realizar experimentos por sí mismos, lo que podría cambiar la forma de hacer investigación.

**Qué pasó** – Un robot científico llamado Eve, construido en el Reino Unido y ahora trabajando en Suecia, puede probar rápidamente muchos químicos para encontrar nuevos medicamentos. Eve descubrió una forma de combatir la malaria al probar 1,600 químicos sin ayuda humana. Otros robots como Adam y Genesis también se están desarrollando para estudiar genes y moléculas. Algunos laboratorios usan robots con IA que entienden instrucciones en lenguaje sencillo y pueden planear experimentos por sí solos.

**Impacto** – Estos laboratorios autónomos pueden hacer experimentos más rápido y barato que los humanos. Por ejemplo, un robot probó más de 30,000 condiciones y ayudó a reducir costos en un 40% mientras mejoraba la producción de proteínas. Los robots pueden realizar tareas complejas como observar experimentos con cámaras y ajustar pasos, lo que hace la investigación más eficiente y precisa.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores trabajan para que estos laboratorios sean totalmente autónomos, donde los robots manejen casi todas las partes de los experimentos. Están mejorando las habilidades de los robots para hacer tareas más complejas y esperan usarlos para desarrollar nuevos medicamentos y materiales más rápido. Algunas empresas ya están creando grandes laboratorios robotizados para ofrecer servicios a otros investigadores.

**Frase clave** – Los robots con IA que gestionan laboratorios autónomos están transformando la investigación científica al hacer los experimentos más rápidos, económicos y precisos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a large, intricate robotic arm gently assembling molecular structures on a glowing laboratory table inside a spacious, softly lit room symbolizing a futuristic lab, with abstract representations of chemical compounds and data streams flowing around it in calm, natural hues of blues, greens, and soft golds.

articles.title: Quantum simulations verified by experiments for the first time
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00959-1Physicists cross-checked quantum computer predictions against experimental data about materials' properties.
articles.content:
Neutron-scattering experiments that helped to verify the quantum predictions were carried out at Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Tennessee. Credit: Oak Ridge National Laboratory

The hope for quantum computers is that the devices will be able to solve complex tasks such as predicting how chemicals react or cracking encrypted text. One of the main reasons that the machines are not yet living up to that potential is the fact that their error rates are high.

Now, for the first time, physicists have matched detailed simulations done on quantum computers to experimental data gathered from work with solid materials. The research shows how the results of quantum simulations can be tested with real-world data — a capability that will become increasingly important as these machines begin to make calculations that are beyond those that ordinary supercomputers can manage. Two teams achieved the results independently, and the work is described in two preprints posted on the arXiv server in the past two weeks. These are yet to be peer reviewed.

IBM quantum computer passes calculation milestone

The experiments were done with the explicit aim “to contrast the data with what we’re measuring in the quantum computer”, says Alexandre Dauphin, a physicist at a quantum-computing start-up called Pasqal in Paris and the lead author of one of the studies1.

Arnab Banerjee, a physicist at Purdue University in West Lafayette, Indiana, who led the second study2, says that cross-checks, or benchmarks, using real materials that can be extensively analysed in the laboratory are crucial “so you know what you are simulating really makes sense”.

Analog versus digital

The simulation of natural quantum phenomena — such as that underlying some materials’ ability to conduct electricity without any resistance — has long been described as one of the main potential applications of quantum computers. Researchers hope that using the computers as virtual laboratories will enable them to understand such phenomena, and will guide the creation of next-generation materials and chemicals, including drugs.

Dauphin and his collaborators simulated a magnetic material that contains the rare element thulium. The material has a crystal structure in which the atoms are unable to align their magnetic orientations in an ordered way, and was predicted to have complex patterns of quantum interactions. To mimic the physics of this crystal, the researchers used one of Pasqal’s ‘neutral atom’ quantum computers, which encode information in the quantum states of individual atoms held with laser light in ‘optical tweezers’. Using an approach called analog quantum simulation, the Pasqal quantum computer calculated properties such as the material’s heat capacity — how much energy it takes to increase the temperature of one gram by 1 ºC — and how it responds to changing magnetic fields.

Daniel González-Cuadra, a theoretical physicist at the Institute for Theoretical Physics in Madrid, says that benchmarking the model against experimental measurements of the actual materials is “sets the stage for a new standard in the application of quantum simulation to materials science.”

Results of a neutron-scattering experiment (left) and an IBM quantum-computer-aided simulation of the experiment (right).Credit: Y.-T. Lee et al./arXiv

Banerjee’s team simulated a different material, made of copper, fluorine and potassium, which also contains atoms that are not magnetically aligned. Like the material simulated by Dauphin’s team, this was predicted to have complex patterns of quantum interactions.

The team implemented a scheme called digital quantum simulation on an IBM quantum computer that stores information inside superconducting loops of metal instead of in individual atoms. The researchers simulated the material’s response to being excited into a range of energy states, as well as the appearance of ‘fractional’ electrons — in which the material’s electrons behave collectively as if they have only a portion of their regular magnetism (which would be impossible for a single particle).

Each simulation had different strengths, Dauphin and Banerjee say. Both teams compared their predictions with data from neutron-scattering experiments, which reveal hidden features of a material by analysing how neutrons scatter off it and how their energy changes.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough where quantum computer simulations were experimentally verified for the first time, demonstrating a crucial step toward reliable quantum computing applications with broad implications for materials science and technology development.
articles.personality_title: Quantum computers’ predictions confirmed by real experiments for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers are special machines that scientists hope will solve very difficult problems, like understanding how chemicals react or breaking secret codes. However, these computers often make mistakes, so researchers need ways to check if their predictions are correct.

**What happened** – For the first time, two groups of scientists used quantum computers to simulate the behavior of complex materials and then compared these simulations to real experiments. One team in Paris used a quantum computer that works with single atoms trapped by lasers to study a magnetic material containing thulium. Another team in Indiana used an IBM quantum computer based on superconducting circuits to simulate a different material made of copper, fluorine, and potassium. Both groups matched their computer results with data from neutron-scattering experiments, which help reveal the materials’ hidden properties.

**Impact** – This is the first successful test showing that quantum computer simulations can be trusted by comparing them directly with real-world experiments. This step is important because it proves quantum computers can accurately model materials, which might lead to designing new materials and medicines in the future. It also helps scientists know when the quantum computers are working correctly.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue improving quantum computers and their simulations to study even more complicated materials and phenomena. As these machines get better, they could solve problems that regular supercomputers cannot, opening new possibilities in science and technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have for the first time confirmed that quantum computer simulations match real experiments, marking a key advance in making quantum computing reliable for studying materials.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les prédictions des ordinateurs quantiques confirmées par des expériences réelles pour la première fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont des machines spéciales que les scientifiques espèrent utiliser pour résoudre des problèmes très complexes, comme comprendre les réactions chimiques ou déchiffrer des codes secrets. Cependant, ces ordinateurs font souvent des erreurs, donc les chercheurs doivent vérifier si leurs prédictions sont justes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour la première fois, deux équipes de scientifiques ont utilisé des ordinateurs quantiques pour simuler le comportement de matériaux complexes, puis ont comparé ces simulations à de vraies expériences. Une équipe à Paris a utilisé un ordinateur quantique qui manipule des atomes piégés par des lasers pour étudier un matériau magnétique contenant du thulium. Une autre équipe en Indiana a utilisé un ordinateur quantique IBM basé sur des circuits supraconducteurs pour simuler un autre matériau composé de cuivre, fluor et potassium. Les deux groupes ont comparé leurs résultats aux données d’expériences de diffusion de neutrons, qui révèlent les propriétés cachées des matériaux.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un test réussi montre que les simulations sur ordinateur quantique peuvent être fiables en les comparant directement avec des expériences réelles. Cette avancée est importante car elle prouve que les ordinateurs quantiques peuvent modéliser avec précision les matériaux, ce qui pourrait aider à concevoir de nouveaux matériaux et médicaments à l’avenir. Cela aide aussi les scientifiques à savoir quand les ordinateurs quantiques fonctionnent correctement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer les ordinateurs quantiques et leurs simulations pour étudier des matériaux et des phénomènes encore plus complexes. Lorsque ces machines deviendront plus performantes, elles pourront résoudre des problèmes que les superordinateurs classiques ne peuvent pas, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités en science et technologie.

**Phrase clé** – Pour la première fois, les scientifiques ont confirmé que les simulations d’ordinateurs quantiques correspondent à des expériences réelles, marquant une avancée clé vers la fiabilité de l’informatique quantique pour l’étude des matériaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Las predicciones de los ordenadores cuánticos confirmadas por experimentos reales por primera vez
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los ordenadores cuánticos son máquinas especiales que los científicos esperan usar para resolver problemas muy difíciles, como entender cómo reaccionan los químicos o descifrar códigos secretos. Sin embargo, estos ordenadores a menudo cometen errores, por lo que los investigadores necesitan formas de comprobar si sus predicciones son correctas.

**Qué pasó** – Por primera vez, dos grupos de científicos usaron ordenadores cuánticos para simular el comportamiento de materiales complejos y luego compararon estas simulaciones con experimentos reales. Un equipo en París usó un ordenador cuántico que trabaja con átomos individuales atrapados por láseres para estudiar un material magnético que contiene tulio. Otro equipo en Indiana usó un ordenador cuántico de IBM basado en circuitos superconductores para simular un material diferente hecho de cobre, flúor y potasio. Ambos grupos compararon sus resultados con datos de experimentos de dispersión de neutrones, que ayudan a revelar las propiedades ocultas de los materiales.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera prueba exitosa que muestra que las simulaciones de ordenadores cuánticos pueden ser confiables al compararlas directamente con experimentos del mundo real. Este paso es importante porque demuestra que los ordenadores cuánticos pueden modelar materiales con precisión, lo que podría llevar a diseñar nuevos materiales y medicamentos en el futuro. También ayuda a los científicos a saber cuándo los ordenadores cuánticos están funcionando correctamente.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán mejorando los ordenadores cuánticos y sus simulaciones para estudiar materiales y fenómenos aún más complicados. A medida que estas máquinas mejoren, podrían resolver problemas que los superordenadores normales no pueden, abriendo nuevas posibilidades en la ciencia y la tecnología.

**Frase clave** – Por primera vez, los científicos han confirmado que las simulaciones de ordenadores cuánticos coinciden con experimentos reales, marcando un avance clave para que la computación cuántica sea confiable en el estudio de materiales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two interconnected abstract crystalline structures—one glowing softly with clusters of tiny, illuminated atoms held gently by delicate beams of light representing optical tweezers, and the other composed of smooth, looping metallic circuits symbolizing superconducting quantum bits—surrounded by swirling, translucent waves symbolizing neutron scattering, all set against a calm, softly blended natural color background that evokes a sense of harmony between quantum simulation and experimental validation.

articles.title: Scientists stretched a liquid and it snapped like a solid
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered something that seems almost impossible: under the right conditions, ordinary liquids can snap apart like solid objects. In experiments, researchers found that when certain liquids are stretched with enough force, they don’t just thin and flow—they suddenly fracture with a sharp break, much like metal under stress. This surprising behavior appears to be tied to viscosity, not elasticity, challenging long-held assumptions about how liquids behave.
articles.content:
In a surprising discovery that could reshape fluid mechanics, researchers at Drexel University have shown that under certain conditions, a simple liquid can behave like a solid and fracture. The study, published in Physical Review Letters, demonstrates that viscous liquids can abruptly snap when stretched with enough force.

This finding suggests that viscosity, or a liquid's resistance to flow, plays a much larger role in its mechanical behavior than scientists previously believed. It also opens the door to new ways of controlling liquids in applications ranging from hydraulics and 3D printing to blood flow in the body.

"Our findings show that if pulled apart with enough force per area, a simple liquid -- a liquid that flows -- will reach what we call a point of 'critical stress,' when it will actually fracture like a solid. And this is likely true for all simple liquids, including common examples, such as water and oil," said Thamires Lima, PhD, an assistant research professor in Drexel's College of Engineering, who helped to lead the research. "This fundamentally changes our understanding of fluid dynamics."

A Surprising Snap During Testing

The discovery came unexpectedly while Lima and her team were studying two simple liquids in collaboration with ExxonMobil Technology & Engineering Company. During an extensional rheology test -- which measures how much force is needed to make a liquid flow -- the researchers observed something unusual. Instead of stretching and thinning like honey, the tar-like liquids suddenly snapped apart.

"What we observed was so unexpected that we needed to repeat the experiments a few more times to make sure it was real," said Nicolas Alvarez, PhD, a professor in the College of Engineering whose lab led the research. "Once we confirmed the phenomenon, the research became an entirely different scientific endeavor."

Using a high-speed camera, the team captured behavior typically seen in solid materials. When stretched, solids elongate until they reach a critical stress point, then break suddenly in a process known as brittle fracture. According to the researchers, this type of fracture had never before been observed in a simple liquid.

"This was an incredibly surprising thing to behold," Lima said. "The fracture caused a very loud snapping noise that actually startled me. I thought at first the machine had broken, but soon realized that the noise came from the stretching fluid."

Viscosity and the Critical Breaking Point

The first liquids to exhibit this behavior were tar-like hydrocarbon blends, which fractured at a critical stress of 2 megaPascals -- roughly the force you might feel if a laundry bag filled with 10 bricks snagged on your fingernail while falling.

To explore further, the researchers tested another simple liquid, styrene oligomer, with the same viscosity. It fractured under the same stretching conditions, indicating that viscosity is a key factor in this solid-like breaking behavior and suggesting that many simple liquids may share a similar breaking point.

The team then adjusted temperature to change viscosity. At each level, they found a specific stretching rate that triggered fracture, always tied to the same 2 megaPascal critical stress. At lower viscosities, the liquids could not be broken because the testing equipment could not stretch them fast enough.

Challenging Long-Held Assumptions

Until now, fracture has been considered a property of elasticity, which is a material's ability to store and withstand stress. Simple liquids, however, do not typically store stress in this way. Instead, they flow when force is applied, rather than bending or breaking.

In most cases, elasticity only becomes relevant when a liquid is cooled below its "glass transition," the point where it begins to behave more like a solid. Observing fracture in liquids that are still fully in their liquid state shows that breaking is not limited to elastic materials.

"Although viscoelastic and polymer liquids -- things like Oobleck or homemade slime -- have demonstrated solid-like fracture behavior, simple liquids have always been thought to exhibit continuous deformation at temperatures above their glass transition and therefore would not fracture," Lima said. "Showing that viscous effects are enough to promote solid-like fracture behavior opens a world of new questions to explore in this area of scientific inquiry."

A Broad and Unexpected Phenomenon

The researchers also compared a simple liquid, oligomer styrene, with a related polymer liquid. Both broke at the same critical stress point, suggesting that elasticity is not responsible for the fracture behavior in simple liquids.

"This suggests that many other elastic liquids might also break at a relatively similar critical stress point," Lima said. "This points to a phenomenon that is relatively chemistry independent and possibly generalizable to a wide range of liquids."

What Causes Liquids to Fracture

The team plans to continue investigating why this happens and how widespread the effect may be. Early evidence points to cavitation -- a process where tiny vapor bubbles form and collapse rapidly, generating shockwaves within the liquid -- as a possible explanation.

"Now that we have reported this unanticipated behavior, the work of fully understanding why it happens and how the behavior manifests in other liquids is an important next step," Lima said. "It will also be interesting to see how this finding may be applied to assist fiber spinning and other applications that use viscous liquids."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a novel scientific discovery that challenges existing understanding of fluid mechanics by showing that simple liquids can fracture like solids under certain conditions. This breakthrough has broad implications for multiple applications such as hydraulics, 3D printing, and medical science, representing a significant advancement with potential large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover liquids can snap like solids under strong stretch
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long believed that simple liquids like water or oil flow smoothly and do not break like solids. Usually, breaking or snapping is something only solid materials do when stretched too far.

**What happened** – Researchers at Drexel University found that when certain liquids are pulled with enough force, they suddenly snap or fracture, just like a solid breaking. This was first seen in thick, tar-like liquids during tests that measure how liquids stretch. The liquids made a loud snap sound when they broke, surprising the scientists.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how we understand liquids. It shows that a liquid’s thickness, called viscosity, is important in how it behaves under stress. The finding suggests many common liquids might break if stretched hard enough, which was not known before. This could affect how we use liquids in machines, 3D printers, or even how blood flows in the body.

**What's next step** – The scientists want to learn why this snapping happens. They think it might be caused by tiny bubbles forming and popping inside the liquid. They will study more liquids to see if they also snap and explore how this knowledge can help improve technology that uses liquids.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered that thick liquids can suddenly snap like solids when stretched hard, revealing new behavior that could change how liquids are used in many technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent que les liquides peuvent se casser comme des solides sous forte tension
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – On pensait depuis longtemps que des liquides simples comme l'eau ou l'huile s'écoulaient sans se casser, contrairement aux solides qui peuvent se briser quand on les étire trop.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'université Drexel ont découvert que certains liquides se cassent soudainement, comme des solides, lorsqu'on les étire avec assez de force. Ce phénomène a été observé pour la première fois sur des liquides épais ressemblant au goudron, qui ont produit un bruit de claquement en se rompant.

**Impact** – Cette découverte change notre compréhension des liquides. Elle montre que la viscosité, c’est-à-dire l'épaisseur du liquide, joue un rôle important dans son comportement sous tension. Cela signifie que beaucoup de liquides courants pourraient se casser s’ils sont assez étirés, ce qui était inconnu jusqu’ici. Cela pourrait influencer l’utilisation des liquides dans les machines, l’impression 3D ou même la circulation sanguine.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent comprendre pourquoi ce claquement se produit. Ils pensent que cela pourrait venir de petites bulles qui se forment et éclatent à l’intérieur du liquide. Ils vont étudier d’autres liquides pour voir s’ils se cassent aussi et chercher comment utiliser cette découverte pour améliorer des technologies.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont découvert que des liquides épais peuvent soudainement se casser comme des solides quand on les étire fort, révélant un nouveau comportement qui pourrait changer l’usage des liquides dans de nombreuses technologies.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren que los líquidos pueden romperse como sólidos al estirarlos fuerte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, se pensó que líquidos simples como el agua o el aceite fluían sin romperse, a diferencia de los sólidos que se quiebran cuando se estiran demasiado.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Drexel descubrieron que algunos líquidos pueden romperse de repente, como los sólidos, cuando se estiran con suficiente fuerza. Esto se observó primero en líquidos espesos parecidos al alquitrán, que hicieron un fuerte sonido al romperse.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo cambia la forma en que entendemos los líquidos. Muestra que la viscosidad, o grosor del líquido, es importante en cómo se comporta bajo tensión. Esto sugiere que muchos líquidos comunes podrían romperse si se estiran lo suficiente, algo que no se sabía antes. Esto puede afectar el uso de líquidos en máquinas, impresión 3D o incluso en el flujo de sangre.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren entender por qué ocurre esta ruptura. Creen que puede ser por burbujas pequeñas que se forman y revientan dentro del líquido. Planean estudiar más líquidos para ver si también se rompen y buscar cómo usar este descubrimiento para mejorar tecnologías.

**Frase clave** – Científicos descubrieron que líquidos espesos pueden romperse de repente como sólidos al estirarlos fuerte, revelando un nuevo comportamiento que podría cambiar el uso de líquidos en muchas tecnologías.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a thick, amber-colored viscous liquid strand being gently stretched between two smooth, glowing spheres, captured mid-snap with a delicate, sharp fracture line glowing softly at the break, set against a warm, muted background of soft earth tones that evoke a calm scientific discovery atmosphere.

articles.title: Anthony Leggett obituary: physicist who brought quantum theory to the macro world
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 30 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01010-zThe polymath also trained as a philosopher and won a Nobel prize for his theory of superfluids.
articles.content:
Credit: L. Brian Stauffer, University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign

Originally educated as a philosopher, Anthony (Tony) Leggett later switched to theoretical condensed-matter physics, and rapidly made the subject of macroscopic quantum phenomena his own. Only eight years after his PhD, he created a remarkably intuitive theory of superfluids. For this work, he was awarded the 2003 Nobel Prize in Physics. He is best known, however, for the creation of a theory that has enabled scientists to understand and harness quantum phenomena in computing. He has died aged 87.

Major Turing computing award goes to quantum science for first time

Born in 1938 in London, Leggett showed early talent in a variety of subjects, and went to Balliol College at the University of Oxford, UK, to study ‘Greats’ (philosophy, ancient languages and history). He was disappointed with the style of academic philosophy at Oxford, so after his degree, he changed focus. Benefiting from the space race — kicked off by the launch of Sputnik 1 in 1957, which prompted Western countries to encourage scholars to switch to science — he acquired funding for a second degree at Oxford, in physics. He continued on to do a PhD with Dirk ter Haar in condensed-matter theory. He later remarked that ter Haar’s hands-off supervision approach was advantageous for him. It enabled him to work on what he wanted, and to develop his independent style. During this time, Leggett also began to learn some of the many languages he became fluent in.

Leggett’s postdoctoral studies at the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign (UIUC) and at Kyoto University, Japan, were a revelation for him. He developed a unique theoretical style, combining the empirically based theory and model-building that were popular in Urbana with the pervasive influence of the Moscow-based Landau school of theoretical physics, which treated physics as a unified whole. This influenced much of his later work, which eschewed excessive mathematics and emphasized physical principles.

In 1967, Leggett started his first academic position at the University of Sussex near Brighton, UK, where he took on a huge teaching load, as well as supervising PhD students from around the world (including myself). He maintained this teaching load until he left the university in 1982. After an eight-month sabbatical at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York, he spent the rest of his career at UIUC.

Einstein hated entanglement — and five other quantum myths

It was during his years at Sussex that Leggett did the two pieces of work for which he is best known. First, between 1972 and 1976, he produced a complete theory of the newly discovered phases of an isotope of helium called liquid helium-3. Leggett recognized that these phases were superfluid, flowing without friction. Unlike superfluid helium-4, the 3He phases were extremely complex, and were described by a complicated mathematical framework. Leggett saw through all this to create a surprisingly simple theory of their behaviour. Among other predictions, he showed that studies of these phases using nuclear magnetic resonance would produce a bizarre and large signal — which was quickly confirmed by experiments.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significant scientific contributions of Anthony Leggett, whose pioneering quantum theories have broad implications for quantum computing and condensed-matter physics. His work has tangible, large-scale benefits for science and technology, inspiring progress in understanding quantum phenomena with meaningful societal impact.
articles.personality_title: Anthony Leggett’s quantum theories changed how we understand the physical world
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Anthony Leggett was a scientist who started as a philosopher but later became a physicist. He focused on understanding how quantum physics works in large systems, which was a big challenge in science.

**What happened** – Leggett created an important theory about superfluids, a type of fluid that can flow without friction, especially in the isotope helium-3. His work helped explain strange behaviors in these fluids and earned him the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2003. He also helped develop ideas that support quantum computing. Leggett passed away at the age of 87.

**Impact** – His theories made it possible for scientists to study and use quantum effects on a much larger scale than before. This is important because quantum effects are usually very tiny and hard to see. Thanks to his work, new technologies like quantum computers can be developed, which may solve problems regular computers cannot.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue building on Leggett’s ideas to improve quantum technologies. His clear and practical approach to physics serves as a guide for future discoveries in quantum science and computing.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Anthony Leggett’s work opened the door for understanding and using quantum physics beyond tiny particles, influencing modern technology and science.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les théories quantiques d’Anthony Leggett ont changé notre compréhension du monde physique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Anthony Leggett était un scientifique qui a commencé comme philosophe avant de devenir physicien. Il s’est concentré sur la compréhension du fonctionnement de la physique quantique dans des systèmes de grande taille, un défi important pour la science.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Leggett a créé une théorie importante sur les superfluides, un type de fluide qui peut s’écouler sans friction, notamment dans l’isotope hélium-3. Son travail a aidé à expliquer des comportements étranges de ces fluides et lui a valu le prix Nobel de physique en 2003. Il a aussi contribué au développement des idées soutenant l’informatique quantique. Leggett est décédé à l’âge de 87 ans.

**Impact** – Ses théories ont permis aux scientifiques d’étudier et d’utiliser les effets quantiques à une échelle beaucoup plus grande qu’avant. C’est important car les effets quantiques sont normalement très petits et difficiles à observer. Grâce à son travail, de nouvelles technologies comme les ordinateurs quantiques peuvent être développées, capables de résoudre des problèmes que les ordinateurs classiques ne peuvent pas.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à s’appuyer sur les idées de Leggett pour améliorer les technologies quantiques. Son approche claire et pratique de la physique sert de guide pour les futures découvertes en science et informatique quantique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le travail d’Anthony Leggett a ouvert la voie à la compréhension et à l’utilisation de la physique quantique au-delà des particules minuscules, influençant la technologie et la science modernes.
articles.personality_title_es: Las teorías cuánticas de Anthony Leggett cambiaron cómo entendemos el mundo físico
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Anthony Leggett fue un científico que comenzó como filósofo y luego se convirtió en físico. Se enfocó en entender cómo funciona la física cuántica en sistemas grandes, lo cual era un gran reto para la ciencia.

**Qué pasó** – Leggett creó una teoría importante sobre los superfluidos, un tipo de fluido que puede fluir sin fricción, especialmente en el isótopo helio-3. Su trabajo ayudó a explicar comportamientos extraños en estos fluidos y le valió el Premio Nobel de Física en 2003. También ayudó a desarrollar ideas que apoyan la computación cuántica. Leggett falleció a los 87 años.

**Impacto** – Sus teorías permitieron a los científicos estudiar y usar efectos cuánticos a una escala mucho mayor que antes. Esto es importante porque los efectos cuánticos suelen ser muy pequeños y difíciles de ver. Gracias a su trabajo, se pueden desarrollar nuevas tecnologías como las computadoras cuánticas, que pueden resolver problemas que las computadoras normales no pueden.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores continuarán construyendo sobre las ideas de Leggett para mejorar las tecnologías cuánticas. Su enfoque claro y práctico de la física sirve como guía para futuros descubrimientos en ciencia y computación cuántica.

**Frase clave** – El trabajo de Anthony Leggett abrió la puerta para entender y usar la física cuántica más allá de las partículas pequeñas, influyendo en la tecnología y la ciencia modernas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a flowing, translucent liquid helium-3 stream gently swirling above an open vintage book symbolizing philosophy and knowledge, with delicate, glowing quantum wave patterns subtly woven into the liquid’s curves, set against a simple, softly colored background evoking a serene academic study.
